schools.summary: Gill: Paul, having removed the charge of levity and inconstancy, goes on to excuse his delay in coming and to soften the severity some thought he had used in his former epistle; he resolved within himself not to come in heaviness; Matthew Henry: Paul proceeds in giving an account of his conduct; Paul declaring that he had determined with himself not to come again to them in heaviness — the loving resolve behind the delay (Paul deliberately put off his visit rather than come while matters stood such that his coming must bring grief, a painful visit of rebuke, choosing to wait until they had amended, that his coming might be joyful not sorrowful).

themes: I determined this with myself
not come again to you in heaviness
the loving resolve behind the delay
waiting that his coming might be joyful

schools.summary: Barnes: if, when Paul should come among them, he should be called on to inflict sorrow by punishing the offending brethren by an act of severe discipline, who would there be to give him comfort but those very persons whom he had affected with grief?; Paul reasoning that, if he makes them sorry, the only ones to make him glad are those he has grieved — the bond of mutual feeling (Paul's joy and the church's so intertwined that, were he to grieve them, he would rob himself of the very gladness they alone could give, so that to wound them would be to wound his own joy, the reasoning showing his love).

themes: if I make you sorry
who... maketh me glad, but the same which is made sorry
the bond of mutual feeling
grieving them would wound his own joy

schools.summary: Gill: Paul wrote (in his former epistle, concerning the excommunication of the incestuous man, which had grieved both him and them) rather than come in person, hoping to hear of their repentance, that he might not have sorrow from those who should be his joy; Paul explaining that he wrote rather than came, lest when he came he should have sorrow from those of whom he ought to rejoice, confident that his joy is the joy of all of them — the loving reason for the letter (Paul wrote the painful letter to give them opportunity to amend, so that when he came he would find joy not grief, trusting that what gladdens him gladdens them too, their joy being one).

themes: I wrote this same unto you
lest... I should have sorrow from them of whom I ought to rejoice
my joy is the joy of you all
the painful letter to give room for amendment

schools.summary: Gill: Paul wrote out of much affliction and anguish of heart, being greatly pressed in his spirit and grieved at heart for the abominable iniquities among them, which they seemed to take no notice of and be unconcerned about, yea rather puffed up; Paul declaring that he wrote with many tears, not to grieve them but to show the love he had more abundantly to them — the painful letter as an act of love (no cold rebuke but wrung from a breaking heart and written through tears, its aim not their pain but their good, the very severity a proof of his deep affection, grieving them only to do them good).

themes: out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote... with many tears
not that ye should be grieved
that ye might know the love which I have... unto you
the severity a proof of love

schools.summary: Gill: the incestuous person is meant (though not named), who had been the cause and occasion of much grief and sorrow to himself and others; Paul takes it for granted he has caused grief; Matthew Henry draws the case of the offender; Paul observing that, if any has caused grief, he has grieved not Paul only but in part the whole church, yet Paul tempers the charge that he may not overburden them — the gentle handling of the offence (the offender, the man disciplined in the first letter, grieved the whole community as a public scandal, not Paul alone, yet Paul moderates his words, not pressing the blame too hard upon the church, that none be overwhelmed).

themes: if any have caused grief
he hath not grieved me, but in part
that I may not overcharge you all
the gentle handling of the offence

schools.summary: Gill: by this punishment is meant the excommunication or censure laid on the incestuous person by the church; Barnes: Paul's object here is to have him again restored, saying the punishment the church had inflicted was sufficient; Paul declaring that the punishment inflicted by the many (the majority of the church) is sufficient for such a man — the moderation of discipline (the censure the church had laid upon the offender, his excommunication decreed by the majority, had now done its work and was enough, so further severity would be needless and harmful, the discipline aiming at repentance not destruction, and having achieved it).

themes: sufficient to such a man is this punishment
which was inflicted of many
the censure had done its work
discipline aims at repentance, not destruction

schools.summary: Gill: far from inflicting any further punishment or increasing what was inflicted, they ought to forgive him by taking off the censure, which had been sufficient; Barnes notes the danger that he be overwhelmed; Paul urging that they ought rather to forgive and comfort the offender, lest he be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow — the restorative aim of discipline (the censure having worked repentance, the church must now turn from punishing to forgiving and comforting the penitent, lest excessive sorrow drive him to despair, the goal being his recovery not his ruin, discipline restoring not destroying).

themes: ye ought rather to forgive him, and comfort him
lest... swallowed up with overmuch sorrow
the restorative aim of discipline
his recovery, not his ruin

schools.summary: Gill: Paul beseeches (exhorts) them; Barnes: the word rendered 'confirm' (kurōsai, occurring in the New Testament only here and Galatians 3:15) means to give authority, to establish as valid, to confirm — that they should give strong expressions and assurances of their love to him, pursuing such a course as to convince him of their affection; Paul beseeching them to confirm (ratify, publicly assure) their love toward the offender — the public restoration of love (the church, having forgiven the penitent, must now openly and decisively renew its love to him, giving him solid assurance of acceptance, so the forgiveness is not merely felt but formally and warmly expressed, lest he doubt his welcome).

themes: I beseech you
that ye would confirm your love toward him
the public restoration of love
solid assurance of acceptance

schools.summary: Gill: Paul wrote, both in this and his former epistle, with this view; Barnes: Paul does not say this was the only purpose — not that he wished in an arbitrary manner to test them or induce them to do anything, but to prove their willingness to obey; Paul explaining that he wrote to know the proof of them, whether they were obedient in all things — the testing of their obedience (one aim of the painful letter was to prove the Corinthians' submission to apostolic authority, both in disciplining the offender at first and now in forgiving him, their willingness to obey in both the severe and the gentle command being the test).

themes: for to this end also did I write
that I might know the proof of you
whether ye be obedient in all things
obedience in both disciplining and forgiving

schools.summary: Gill: this may extend to the forgiveness of any person in any case, in time past, present, or to come, though it has particular respect to the forgiveness of the incestuous man, which Paul was very ready to come into and join with the church in; Paul declaring that whom they forgive, he forgives also, and that what he forgave, he forgave for their sakes in the person (presence, or stead) of Christ — the unity of apostle and church in forgiveness (Paul joining his pardon to theirs, exercising his forgiveness not for himself but for their good, and doing so as Christ's representative, by his authority and in his presence, so the church's forgiveness carries Christ's own warrant).

themes: to whom ye forgive any thing, I forgive also
for your sakes forgave I it
in the person of Christ
the unity of apostle and church in pardon

schools.summary: Gill: Satan gets an advantage of the churches when church discipline is brought into neglect and contempt, or turned into tyranny, or when he can draw off any person from the church; the metaphor is of a covetous trader who takes every occasion to circumvent and deceive; Paul giving the reason for forgiving — lest Satan get an advantage of them, for they are not ignorant of his devices (schemes, wiles) — the vigilance against the enemy (excessive severity, refusing to restore the penitent, would play into Satan's hand, who would use it to drive the man to despair or harden the church, so the very discipline meant to defeat sin could, if pushed too far, become Satan's tool, the wisdom of moderation defeating his scheme).

themes: lest Satan should get an advantage of us
for we are not ignorant of his devices
excessive severity plays into Satan's hand
vigilance against the enemy

schools.summary: Chrysostom: these words seem on one hand unworthy of Paul, if because of a brother's absence he threw away so great an opportunity of saving, and on the other to hang apart from the context — yet on examination they hang together and he has said nothing unworthy of himself; Paul recounting that, coming to Troas to preach Christ's gospel, a door was opened to him by the Lord — the providential opportunity (a wide and effectual opening for the gospel, a 'door opened of the Lord,' a ripe field for the saving of souls, granted by divine providence not chance), which makes all the more striking the restlessness that follows (v.13, that Paul left even a ripe field).

themes: when I came to Troas to preach Christ's gospel
a door was opened unto me of the Lord
the providential opportunity
a ripe field for souls

schools.summary: Gill: though such a door was opened to preach with success, Paul was greatly distressed in mind, very restless and uneasy in spirit, and could not be satisfied to stay — which shows that, though so great a man, he was but a man, and of like passions with others; Paul declaring that he had no rest in his spirit because he found not Titus (who was to bring news of Corinth), and so left Troas for Macedonia — the human anxiety of the apostle (his concern for the Corinthians so weighing on him that even an open door could not hold him, his restless love driving him on to Macedonia to seek tidings, showing the apostle a man of deep affection and real feeling).

themes: I had no rest in my spirit
because I found not Titus my brother
I went from thence into Macedonia
the restless love of the apostle

schools.summary: Gill: Paul, having mentioned the door opened at Troas, recalls the great and manifold appearances of God for him and his fellow ministers, in blessing their labours to the conversion of many souls, and breaks forth into thanksgiving; Paul giving thanks to God, who always causes them to triumph in Christ and makes manifest the savour of his knowledge in every place — the triumphal procession of the gospel (God, like a victorious general leading a triumph, leads his apostles in Christ's train, spreading everywhere through them the fragrant knowledge of Christ, so the very ministry that brought Paul affliction is a continual victory and a diffusing of the sweet aroma of the gospel).

themes: thanks be unto God, which always causeth us to triumph in Christ
maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge
the triumphal procession of the gospel
the fragrance of Christ spread everywhere

schools.summary: Gill: the ministers are a sweet savour, not in themselves (for they have the same corrupt hearts and natures as others, and complain of them) but as having the gifts and graces of the Spirit, comparable to fragrant and sweet spices, and as preaching Christ; Paul declaring that they are unto God a sweet savour of Christ, both in those who are saved and those who perish — the acceptableness of faithful ministry to God (the apostles' faithful preaching of Christ a fragrant offering well-pleasing to God regardless of how men receive it, the same gospel ascending as a sweet aroma whether its hearers are saved by it or perish in rejecting it, the faithful minister accepted of God whatever the outcome).

themes: we are unto God a sweet savour of Christ
in them that are saved, and in them that perish
the acceptableness of faithful ministry
the gospel a sweet aroma regardless of reception

schools.summary: Gill: to the one (those who are for death, appointed to it) the apostles are the savour of death unto death; what Paul says of the gospel and gospel ministers, the Jews frequently said of the law; Paul declaring that to the one they are the savour of death unto death, to the other the savour of life unto life, and asking who is sufficient for these things — the solemn double effect of the gospel (the same preached Christ becoming, to those who reject him, a deepening condemnation, death unto death, and to those who believe, an ever-increasing life, life unto life, an outcome so weighty, with such eternal consequences, that Paul cries out at the awful responsibility, 'who is sufficient?', none sufficient in himself).

debates.question: How is the same gospel 'a savour of death' to some and 'of life' to others?

sides.position: The same preached Christ deepens the condemnation of those who reject him (death unto death) and increases the life of those who believe (life unto life) — the gospel dividing its hearers by their response

themes: the savour of death unto death... the savour of life unto life
who is sufficient for these things?
the twofold effect of the gospel
the awful responsibility of the ministry

schools.summary: Gill: Paul removes from himself a character belonging not to him but to the false apostles, who are described as 'many' — great swarms of false teachers in the early times of Christianity (some copies read 'as the rest'); Paul declaring that they are not as the many who corrupt (adulterate, as a trader corrupts his goods for gain) the word of God, but speak of sincerity (purity, single-heartedness), as of God (God his source), in the sight of God (his presence), in Christ (in union with him, by his authority) — the integrity of the true minister (his ministry marked by transparent honesty before the all-seeing God, no corruption or gain-seeking, against those who adulterate the word).

themes: we are not as many, which corrupt the word of God
but as of sincerity, but as of God
in the sight of God speak we in Christ
the integrity of the true minister
